text: Welcome to Fragmented, a software developer podcast where we talk about building good software and becoming better developers.
speaker: SPEAKER_00

text: My name's Don Felker.
speaker: SPEAKER_01

text: And I'm Kausha Gopal.
speaker: SPEAKER_01

text: Welcome to the show.
speaker: SPEAKER_00

text: Before we get started here today, I wanted to tell you about the relaunch of androidjobs.io.

text: So please go to androidjobs.io and you can post a job for free.
speaker: SPEAKER_00

text: Or if you're looking to see what other jobs are available, you can also go there and plug your email address in
speaker: SPEAKER_00

text: And we'll start emailing you here very soon, letting you know weekly about the new jobs that are available on androidjobs.io.
speaker: SPEAKER_00

text: Again, check it out at androidjobs.io.
speaker: SPEAKER_00

text: Everybody, welcome back to the show.
speaker: SPEAKER_00

text: This week we have something a little bit short, but it's a topic that I wanted to talk about simply because I've been chatting with a number of developers through Twitter and Instagram and YouTube all over the place.
speaker: SPEAKER_00

text: And I've had a lot of discussions and also dealt with this a lot in my professional career as someone who's been consulting for close to 15 to 20 years now.
speaker: SPEAKER_00

text: And those things I'm gonna talk about are the three things that every software developer needs to know how to do.
speaker: SPEAKER_00

text: And none of these are really groundbreaking, but they are something that can really help you on your day-to-day process in any type of software project.
speaker: SPEAKER_00

text: So this doesn't strictly apply to mobile developers.
speaker: SPEAKER_00

text: or any type of developer for that regard.
speaker: SPEAKER_00

text: It could apply to mobile or web, could apply to someone who's building a car stereo API in C or some low-level language.
speaker: SPEAKER_00

text: All of this really applies.
speaker: SPEAKER_00

text: So without further ado, let's just hop into it.
speaker: SPEAKER_00

text: So what are the three things that every software developer needs to know how to do?
speaker: SPEAKER_00

text: Number one, you need to know how to identify the premature optimization and avoid it.
speaker: SPEAKER_00

text: So what is premature optimization and what does it look like?
speaker: SPEAKER_00

text: Premature optimization is when you're trying to optimize a piece of code because you think it needs to run a certain way.
speaker: SPEAKER_00

text: It needs to be more performant than the perhaps naive way that you're implementing this.
speaker: SPEAKER_00

text: One really good example of this is perhaps you're taking a very long string or a number of strings and you need to concatenate them together to form one string.
speaker: SPEAKER_00

text: The common knowledge among the software community is that string concatenation in most languages is a slow process.
speaker: SPEAKER_00

text: So you shouldn't use any type of just general string concatenation where you're just concatenating the strings together with the plus operator or whatever the method is, the language that you're using.
speaker: SPEAKER_00

text: You should use something like a string builder or a string buffer or whatever your language is that has the optimization for string concatenation.
speaker: SPEAKER_00

text: This happens a lot.
speaker: SPEAKER_00

text: I see this a lot in code where there'll be a little function that will build a string.
speaker: SPEAKER_00

text: And inside of that function, I'm going to use string builder as an example.
speaker: SPEAKER_00

text: It uses a string builder, which is a performant way to build strings in a number of languages.
speaker: SPEAKER_00

text: And inside of this function, it'll use a string builder.
speaker: SPEAKER_00

text: And what will happen is it's maybe only concatenating five strings.
speaker: SPEAKER_00

text: But the developer who wrote it did it this way because they heard that this is the proper way to build a string that's very performant.
speaker: SPEAKER_00

text: However, it's more code to maintain than just concatenating five strings together on one line of code.
speaker: SPEAKER_00

text: And it could possibly not even give any benefit whatsoever that's perceived to the end user.
speaker: SPEAKER_00

text: And frankly, could be zero benefit perceived to the application whatsoever.
speaker: SPEAKER_00

text: And to give you a further example, let's assume that you have a class, or excuse me, a page or a screen in your application that's the about screen and you need to concatenate five or 10 strings together to display some information on that about screen.
speaker: SPEAKER_00

text: As most of us know who are developers, the about screen of any application is probably the most least visited screen in any application.
speaker: SPEAKER_00

text: No one really cares that much about the application.
speaker: SPEAKER_00

text: They're there to use it because it provides value to them at some way in their life.
speaker: SPEAKER_00

text: So it's not really accessed that much.
speaker: SPEAKER_00

text: So if you're concatenating five or 10 strings together, do you really need to use something like string builder?
speaker: SPEAKER_00

text: In my opinion, absolutely not.
speaker: SPEAKER_00

text: You don't even need to do it.
speaker: SPEAKER_00

text: Just do whatever's quicker and easier to maintain and get the feature done and ship it.
speaker: SPEAKER_00

text: Now, someone's listening to this, I imagine, and saying, well, yeah, that's great for the about screen and that's perfect for that scenario.
speaker: SPEAKER_00

text: But what about, I have a social media app and we can need to concatenate a string in the main timeline, which is the main part of the application.
speaker: SPEAKER_00

text: And if we concatenate that many strings, I know it's gonna be a problem.
speaker: SPEAKER_00

text: My argument here is,
speaker: SPEAKER_00

text: You don't know.
speaker: SPEAKER_00

text: And that may seem a little arrogant, and it's not meant to intend to sound that way.
speaker: SPEAKER_00

text: But the only way I can say that is the only way that you're going to know is if you've measured it.
speaker: SPEAKER_00

text: And I have a saying that says, what gets measured gets managed.
speaker: SPEAKER_00

text: This is something that I didn't create.
speaker: SPEAKER_00

text: It's a saying I picked up a long time ago that I completely agree with, that we don't know if what we're doing is the right thing unless we have a way to measure it.
speaker: SPEAKER_00

text: So let's assume that same example where we have that string concatenation and we're going to do it on the timeline and we're going to scroll this timeline.
speaker: SPEAKER_00

text: And every time we scroll on the timeline, it has to concatenate these five or 10 strings.
speaker: SPEAKER_00

text: Do we really know if that's the worst part of the application?
speaker: SPEAKER_00

text: Is this non-performant or is the compiler or did the compiler perform some type of optimizations underneath the hood that makes that completely obsolete?
speaker: SPEAKER_00

text: So it makes it doesn't even matter.
speaker: SPEAKER_00

text: We don't really know unless we measure it.
speaker: SPEAKER_00

text: And so you need some type of way to measure that.
speaker: SPEAKER_00

text: And so that's kind of beyond the scope of these three things here.
speaker: SPEAKER_00

text: But if you can measure something and it does show you, hey, that this is the actual problem, then you know, all right, well, then I maybe need to fix that.
speaker: SPEAKER_00

text: So my argument here is don't prematurely optimize until you start measuring things.
speaker: SPEAKER_00

text: So you need to recognize when you're premature optimizing.
speaker: SPEAKER_00

text: This just doesn't go for string concatenation.
speaker: SPEAKER_00

text: This could go for something like optimizing, over-optimizing database queries.
speaker: SPEAKER_00

text: realizing that you need to perform a bunch of different joins and so forth to save the couple extra queries that you might do through your ORM or through whatever.
speaker: SPEAKER_00

text: Maybe you're trying to write some custom SQL just so it's one database query and it's really fast.
speaker: SPEAKER_00

text: But you don't know again if that's really gonna be the problem on this screen or not.
speaker: SPEAKER_00

text: So in my opinion, write it so it works.
speaker: SPEAKER_00

text: Get everything working inside of your current screen, your current feature.
speaker: SPEAKER_00

text: and then see how it operates.
speaker: SPEAKER_00

text: If there's no problems, then why should you have to change it?
speaker: SPEAKER_00

text: If it's not performant, there's no jank or anything like that.
speaker: SPEAKER_00

text: I don't see a reason to have to change that type of thing.
speaker: SPEAKER_00

text: Now also, what you also find a lot of people falling into the trap of is doing something because they heard some expert tell them to do it.
speaker: SPEAKER_00

text: I know I've fallen into this trap numerous times.
speaker: SPEAKER_00

text: especially with the string builder example.
speaker: SPEAKER_00

text: And it's one of those situations where, well, I heard so-and-so say this and I highly respect their opinion and I must be right because they're respected in the industry.
speaker: SPEAKER_00

text: So I have to do it this way.
speaker: SPEAKER_00

text: No, you don't have to do it that way.
speaker: SPEAKER_00

text: I always will argue going forward that you need to get it done, then optimize it.
speaker: SPEAKER_00

text: If you spend too much time prematurely optimizing an application, a feature, a piece of code, you're just wasting time that you don't even know if it's valuable time that you're spending on this.
speaker: SPEAKER_00

text: You may end up finding out that that code runs just fine, but your actual problem happens to be latency with the server.
speaker: SPEAKER_00

text: So you need to solve that perception problem with the user.
speaker: SPEAKER_00

text: It's not actually rendering it to the screen.
speaker: SPEAKER_00

text: It's the API call for some reason is slow.
speaker: SPEAKER_00

text: So maybe you need to do some kind of fancy UI magic to make the user feel like something's happening and so forth.
speaker: SPEAKER_00

text: There's a bunch of different examples of this happening.
speaker: SPEAKER_00

text: And I have an example in my own personal life where this happened to me about 10 years ago.
speaker: SPEAKER_00

text: I was working in the client, a very big client that will remain nameless that everyone has probably used their website.
speaker: SPEAKER_00

text: But I was building a system, which was for a main component will be used on the homepage, which gets millions of visits a day.
speaker: SPEAKER_00

text: And this is a web app and I was writing this code and I was prematurely optimizing this function.
speaker: SPEAKER_00

text: It might even been with strings and a number of other things.
speaker: SPEAKER_00

text: And at the time the lead sat down with me and he was looking over my code and we were pair programming.
speaker: SPEAKER_00

text: And he said, why are you doing that?
speaker: SPEAKER_00

text: And I said, because that's the proper way to do it.
speaker: SPEAKER_00

text: We all know that if we don't do that, then it's going to be slow.
speaker: SPEAKER_00

text: And he just goes, how do you know?
speaker: SPEAKER_00

text: And I couldn't answer the question.
speaker: SPEAKER_00

text: I said, well, that's just common knowledge.
speaker: SPEAKER_00

text: He goes, but how do you know that's the slow part of the app?
speaker: SPEAKER_00

text: I'm like, I eventually realized I was cornered.
speaker: SPEAKER_00

text: I said, I don't know that that's the slow part of the app.
speaker: SPEAKER_00

text: I'm just making an assumption based upon what I've read and common industry knowledge.
speaker: SPEAKER_00

text: He said, okay, well, I want you to undo that.
speaker: SPEAKER_00

text: He goes, and then write it the easy way.
speaker: SPEAKER_00

text: And so I kind of argued with him for a while, and I felt that that was kind of ridiculous, but I did it anyway.
speaker: SPEAKER_00

text: And then what we ended up doing, and he said, look, afterwards, we're gonna run this thing through a performance metric tool, and we're gonna see where the slow parts of this application were.
speaker: SPEAKER_00

text: And I was dead positive that that function that I made simpler and not as performant, you know, the one I had optimized early,
speaker: SPEAKER_00

text: I was positive that was going to show up in this report.
speaker: SPEAKER_00

text: I knew it.
speaker: SPEAKER_00

text: Well, they ran the performance.
speaker: SPEAKER_00

text: I mean, they literally hammered this thing with a performance tool and got a bunch of traces on it.
speaker: SPEAKER_00

text: And the leads went through it and identified like the top 50 places in the app that needed to be refined to speed it up.
speaker: SPEAKER_00

text: My area wasn't even there.
speaker: SPEAKER_00

text: It was halfway down, if not towards the end of that performance.
speaker: SPEAKER_00

text: And at that level, it was so negligible, it didn't even matter.
speaker: SPEAKER_00

text: What were the problems?
speaker: SPEAKER_00

text: Random queries to an API or to a database behind the scenes that weren't indexed.
speaker: SPEAKER_00

text: 80% of the problems were those.
speaker: SPEAKER_00

text: That just happens to be that application.
speaker: SPEAKER_00

text: So we applied indexes and things were working again.
speaker: SPEAKER_00

text: And we didn't even know at that point in time that those indexes were even going to be, that those database queries were going to be something that need to be indexed.
speaker: SPEAKER_00

text: So we didn't know.
speaker: SPEAKER_00

text: We approach this strictly from the fashion of let's build this quickly.
speaker: SPEAKER_00

text: Let's use good patterns and practices, but don't over optimize.
speaker: SPEAKER_00

text: So that's number one, kind of long winded there, but you need to identify premature optimization so you can catch yourself from doing it because you'll waste a lot of time.
speaker: SPEAKER_00

text: And you don't know if it's useful or not.
speaker: SPEAKER_00

text: Now, if you do know that your application is slow in this one area, and you do know that every time you guys do something on a particular screen in that part of the app, if you don't use this optimization, it will just slow to a crawl.
speaker: SPEAKER_00

text: Well, in that case, then yeah, use your optimization.
speaker: SPEAKER_00

text: That makes sense.
speaker: SPEAKER_00

text: But overall, you just need to make sure that you're not doing it before you have any metrics to which to dictate I should be prematurely optimizing or I shouldn't.
speaker: SPEAKER_00

text: So always opt for not prematurely optimizing.
speaker: SPEAKER_00

text: Again, there's a saying in the software community that's premature optimization is the devil.
speaker: SPEAKER_00

text: I would have to agree with that.
speaker: SPEAKER_00

text: So just kind of check to see what you're doing to make sure you're not optimizing early.
speaker: SPEAKER_00

text: okay number two this one is would seem to be kind of common but it's actually not common at all in software and that is number two you need to know how to write tests and what the different types of tests are these tests include unit tests integration tests and end-to-end tests so why do you need to know how to do this i have worked at
speaker: SPEAKER_00

text: countless clients where I show up and there's no unit tests.
speaker: SPEAKER_00

text: There's no integration tests.
speaker: SPEAKER_00

text: There's no end to end tests.
speaker: SPEAKER_00

text: And in fact, if I ask if there's unit tests, every once in a while, I might get a yes.
speaker: SPEAKER_00

text: And then what they would show me would be a end to end test.
speaker: SPEAKER_00

text: And I'd say, that's not an end to, that's not a unit test.
speaker: SPEAKER_00

text: That's an end to end test.
speaker: SPEAKER_00

text: And so I'd have to train the clients on the proper way to test and the different types of testing.
speaker: SPEAKER_00

text: So why do you need to know how to write tests?
speaker: SPEAKER_00

text: Well, very simple.
speaker: SPEAKER_00

text: Testing is what's going to validate that your software does what it does.
speaker: SPEAKER_00

text: It's a parachute.
speaker: SPEAKER_00

text: It's a safety mechanism for your software because
speaker: SPEAKER_00

text: Current me right now, I'm writing software that I feel is very good.
speaker: SPEAKER_00

text: And I'm going to write the software.
speaker: SPEAKER_00

text: And of course, it's great.
speaker: SPEAKER_00

text: I've tested it myself.
speaker: SPEAKER_00

text: But the problem is future me doesn't remember what I did six months ago, maybe even two weeks ago.
speaker: SPEAKER_00

text: Heck, maybe even six hours ago.
speaker: SPEAKER_00

text: I don't know.
speaker: SPEAKER_00

text: So what I need to do is make sure that I provide myself a safety net for future me and for future people on my team, if I have multiple people on my team.
speaker: SPEAKER_00

text: And what is going to happen when I write these tests is anytime I change something, if I inadvertently change something I shouldn't have or changed it the wrong way, a test should break and catch that.
speaker: SPEAKER_00

text: Now this could be...
speaker: SPEAKER_00

text: the input and output of something has changed, that's going to be your unit test, you know, or if two different classes work, you know, if I call into one class and it returns something different, that could be an integration test.
speaker: SPEAKER_00

text: Perhaps I'm changing the function signature.
speaker: SPEAKER_00

text: That would be an integration test.
speaker: SPEAKER_00

text: Tests would break in that regard.
speaker: SPEAKER_00

text: And perhaps an end-to-end test would catch something where I changed an API call somewhere or I changed a serialization or deserialization method somewhere.
speaker: SPEAKER_00

text: And the whole app works fine, except when I run the full integration test to realize that, oops, maybe we didn't change it on the server or maybe we didn't change it somewhere else, somewhere in the pipeline.
speaker: SPEAKER_00

text: And the end-to-end test is going to catch it.
speaker: SPEAKER_00

text: So overall, these tests are going to catch you all over the place and provide you with confidence that you can actually write your code.
speaker: SPEAKER_00

text: I'm sure we've all been in the situation where you write an application or you're writing some code and you need to ship it and you're afraid to ship it.
speaker: SPEAKER_00

text: And the reason why you're afraid to ship it is because you're not sure of what you broke.
speaker: SPEAKER_00

text: You don't know what you don't know, especially on a very large app.
speaker: SPEAKER_00

text: If an app has no tests and you have to write some code in an existing part of the app and change something, for me, I find myself getting very worried about, oh, I don't know what I changed.
speaker: SPEAKER_00

text: I really get freaked out because I could change something that's really critical to the application and I might not even know it.
speaker: SPEAKER_00

text: And I could crash this for the company.
speaker: SPEAKER_00

text: I could crash this for all the users.
speaker: SPEAKER_00

text: Depends on how big the app is, how useful the app is.
speaker: SPEAKER_00

text: In fact, I know many stories of various companies in the Silicon Valley that had, you know, these, these, they called them like the God functions were like, that's function is 2000 miles long.
speaker: SPEAKER_00

text: It works.
speaker: SPEAKER_00

text: Don't touch it.
speaker: SPEAKER_00

text: And it was very impossible to put tests around it.
speaker: SPEAKER_00

text: And I heard this was actually a thing.
speaker: SPEAKER_00

text: This might be, you know, tech folklore for all I know, but I heard this was the case early on at PayPal.
speaker: SPEAKER_00

text: There was a function that
speaker: SPEAKER_00

text: that was very long.
speaker: SPEAKER_00

text: It might've been 2000, 20,000.
speaker: SPEAKER_00

text: I don't know.
speaker: SPEAKER_00

text: It was a long function that was written in there.
speaker: SPEAKER_00

text: And its main purpose was to actually run the payment mechanism.
speaker: SPEAKER_00

text: And that was the core component of the payment flow.
speaker: SPEAKER_00

text: And you didn't touch it because it was very hard to test.
speaker: SPEAKER_00

text: It was almost impossible to test, but it worked.
speaker: SPEAKER_00

text: And anyone who had to get in there was fraught with anxiety simply because they weren't sure what's going to break.
speaker: SPEAKER_00

text: Now, if you have this thing completely covered 100% all edge cases in tests,
speaker: SPEAKER_00

text: Well, that gives you a lot more confidence that you can change something.
speaker: SPEAKER_00

text: Now, if you're not really sure of, let's say you're, I mean, rewind here.
speaker: SPEAKER_00

text: Let me say you're in the situation that I just explained.
speaker: SPEAKER_00

text: You've joined a company as a full-time employee, as a consultant, freelancer, whatever.
speaker: SPEAKER_00

text: And you get to the app and you realize, wow, this app doesn't really have any tests or the feature I have doesn't have any tests.
speaker: SPEAKER_00

text: and I am terrified to change it, what do you do?
speaker: SPEAKER_00

text: Well, you need to write some tests around that.
speaker: SPEAKER_00

text: And that's gonna be an actual end-to-end test or an integration test.
speaker: SPEAKER_00

text: Kind of get a test around the largest, biggest piece of it that you're going to be working with.
speaker: SPEAKER_00

text: Now, it might be a screen that just outputs a certain value.
speaker: SPEAKER_00

text: And this doesn't have to be a perfect test.
speaker: SPEAKER_00

text: And this is outlined perfectly in the book called Working Effectively with Legacy Code by Michael Feathers.
speaker: SPEAKER_00

text: And I'll link that in the show notes.
speaker: SPEAKER_00

text: And that book literally lays out the chapters.
speaker: SPEAKER_00

text: And I've mentioned that book many times in this podcast.
speaker: SPEAKER_00

text: Lays out the chapter titles like, I have a huge function and I don't know what to change and I'm afraid I'm going to break it.
speaker: SPEAKER_00

text: It's chapter titles like that that are inside of there.
speaker: SPEAKER_00

text: I don't know.
speaker: SPEAKER_00

text: I have to change a function and I don't know where to start.
speaker: SPEAKER_00

text: And so it lays out all different types of things.
speaker: SPEAKER_00

text: And what he talks about is literally getting a test around that function.
speaker: SPEAKER_00

text: Maybe it's just a function where you test the input and output.
speaker: SPEAKER_00

text: That's easy.
speaker: SPEAKER_00

text: That's a unit test.
speaker: SPEAKER_00

text: Maybe you need to test it.
speaker: SPEAKER_00

text: When I change this function, any of the consumers of the function are going to be correctly still operating.
speaker: SPEAKER_00

text: So I need to make sure I find out any of those places of where that's being used and
speaker: SPEAKER_00

text: And maybe write tests around all those places.
speaker: SPEAKER_00

text: That could take a long time, but that will give you confidence.
speaker: SPEAKER_00

text: And maybe it's actually, you can't even do that.
speaker: SPEAKER_00

text: Maybe it's just impossible to even write a test.
speaker: SPEAKER_00

text: It's such spaghetti code.
speaker: SPEAKER_00

text: What do you do then?
speaker: SPEAKER_00

text: Well, what would a UI tester do?
speaker: SPEAKER_00

text: They would run the app and they would see what the output was.
speaker: SPEAKER_00

text: So maybe you can just run the app, grab a screenshot and check the output.
speaker: SPEAKER_00

text: Now that's not a perfect test.
speaker: SPEAKER_00

text: It's repeatable, but that will give you a little bit of confidence to know as you're changing things.
speaker: SPEAKER_00

text: All right, as I run this, cool.
speaker: SPEAKER_00

text: Is the output the same?
speaker: SPEAKER_00

text: Cool.
speaker: SPEAKER_00

text: If I run this, is the output the same if I change this?
speaker: SPEAKER_00

text: So those things work.
speaker: SPEAKER_00

text: Now, one of the things that you should also mention, I should mention here is if you get into these weird situations where you have to write tests around something just to give yourself confidence to change it, realize that those tests don't have to stay around forever.
speaker: SPEAKER_00

text: That once you refactor the code and get it into a more malleable state, maybe introduce some interfaces, maybe introduce some type of dependency breaking mechanisms, which again are all covered in the working effectively with legacy code book.
speaker: SPEAKER_00

text: At that point, maybe I can actually start running some much more fine grain tests and get rid of that one really big clunky test that gave me confidence before.
speaker: SPEAKER_00

text: That's okay.
speaker: SPEAKER_00

text: I do that quite a bit.
speaker: SPEAKER_00

text: In fact, what usually ends up happening is I'll start with an end-to-end test if a client doesn't have one.
speaker: SPEAKER_00

text: The first test I'll write is, does the application just start?
speaker: SPEAKER_00

text: After we build it, can I start the application and hit the main page and can I read some text off the screen?
speaker: SPEAKER_00

text: For a mobile app, that might be the homepage or the login page.
speaker: SPEAKER_00

text: Does the login page even render?
speaker: SPEAKER_00

text: Does it crash?
speaker: SPEAKER_00

text: That's my first test.
speaker: SPEAKER_00

text: The next one might be, can I log in?
speaker: SPEAKER_00

text: Now I'm not testing the actual functions and anything like that.
speaker: SPEAKER_00

text: I'm just testing, does the app do what it's supposed to do?
speaker: SPEAKER_00

text: Maybe I'm supposed to change some part of the login mechanism where now instead of talking to one server, I have to talk to two for whatever reason.
speaker: SPEAKER_00

text: So I have to split the API calls.
speaker: SPEAKER_00

text: I have to wait for both of them to be returned before I can log someone in.
speaker: SPEAKER_00

text: So I'm going to go write an end to end test before I write my code.
speaker: SPEAKER_00

text: All right, here's what it looks like before I log in.
speaker: SPEAKER_00

text: Here's what it looks like after I log in.
speaker: SPEAKER_00

text: Do I get the same flow when I'm done?
speaker: SPEAKER_00

text: That'll give me confidence to actually write.
speaker: SPEAKER_00

text: the code and make the change now later on i might go back through and refactor and do a bunch of stuff and then be able to test them independently and then i can get rid of that big test and that's fine so just to recap what's we need to know the difference here you know how to write tests and you need to know how to see what the different tests are and know the difference between them
speaker: SPEAKER_00

text: So let's talk about unit tests versus integration tests versus end-to-end tests.
speaker: SPEAKER_00

text: Unit tests usually, and this is going to be paraphrased.
speaker: SPEAKER_00

text: Some people are going to disagree.
speaker: SPEAKER_00

text: That's fine.
speaker: SPEAKER_00

text: This is kind of one of those muddy topics, but this is my interpretation of it.
speaker: SPEAKER_00

text: A unit test is very black box.
speaker: SPEAKER_00

text: This is going to be an input and an output.
speaker: SPEAKER_00

text: I have a function.
speaker: SPEAKER_00

text: The function takes a value and it returns a value.
speaker: SPEAKER_00

text: Does the input and the output do what I expect it to do?
speaker: SPEAKER_00

text: That's a unit test.
speaker: SPEAKER_00

text: I can test this in isolation.
speaker: SPEAKER_00

text: It doesn't need to have...
speaker: SPEAKER_00

text: a cloud working or anything.
speaker: SPEAKER_00

text: There's no external dependencies.
speaker: SPEAKER_00

text: It's very, very isolated.
speaker: SPEAKER_00

text: So it's really easy for me to write these tests.
speaker: SPEAKER_00

text: These tests are going to be the fastest tests simply because they can just fly.
speaker: SPEAKER_00

text: The next one is an integration test.
speaker: SPEAKER_00

text: An integration test is going to be a test where two components are integrating.
speaker: SPEAKER_00

text: Now I can say I'm using the word component here because that could mean two different classes, could mean two different applications, could mean a whole bunch of different stuff.
speaker: SPEAKER_00

text: So it's a very generalized term.
speaker: SPEAKER_00

text: But an integration test, let's just take a simple example is when I call a certain function, I expect that other function to call another service.
speaker: SPEAKER_00

text: Now, what that could mean is I have a, let's consider it a layered architecture.
speaker: SPEAKER_00

text: And when I click the...
speaker: SPEAKER_00

text: In a login class, when the login function is called, I expect it to go call the login service.
speaker: SPEAKER_00

text: And that login service actually has another dependency.
speaker: SPEAKER_00

text: And that one's going to call into the login facade, which then delegates to a bunch of other stuff.
speaker: SPEAKER_00

text: Now, I don't want to test the actual facade, so maybe I've used a mock function to test that out, and I've used mocks, that's fine.
speaker: SPEAKER_00

text: I want to actually test it when I've actually called into the login service, that the login service actually still does call the login facade, because that has to happen for some business reasons.
speaker: SPEAKER_00

text: So I'm just going to check that, hey, did the service get called and did the service actually call into that mock facade there?
speaker: SPEAKER_00

text: And that might be a signature.
speaker: SPEAKER_00

text: I want to make sure that that was called and it should fail.
speaker: SPEAKER_00

text: If I go and comment out that function call to the login facade in the login service, well, now that integration test should fail.
speaker: SPEAKER_00

text: And what that's testing is that did the components integrate correctly?
speaker: SPEAKER_00

text: Am I integrating everything as I expect them to be?
speaker: SPEAKER_00

text: Again, there's going to be some arguments around this about if that's a true integration test.
speaker: SPEAKER_00

text: There's a bunch of different ones out there that you can talk through system test, integration test, whatever.
speaker: SPEAKER_00

text: We're just going to go very general here.
speaker: SPEAKER_00

text: So integration test is anytime you're going to be integrating different components together.
speaker: SPEAKER_00

text: These will usually be pretty fast as well, because if you need to talk to any type of external service, sometimes you'll actually, most of the time in my own experience, I will mock them out and verify that the mock interactions have happened.
speaker: SPEAKER_00

text: And that's me testing the integration.
speaker: SPEAKER_00

text: That's verifying the mock interactions.
speaker: SPEAKER_00

text: Now, what we have at the end here are the end-to-end tests.
speaker: SPEAKER_00

text: Sometimes these are known as system tests.
speaker: SPEAKER_00

text: And these are the tests that will test things from end-to-end.
speaker: SPEAKER_00

text: This is usually going to be, if it's a mobile app, you'll fire up an emulator, you'll actually log in into the app, you will verify you see certain things on the screen, the actual button clicks happen, and you're seeing how things would happen for the end user.
speaker: SPEAKER_00

text: If it's a web app, very similar, but just in the web component, maybe you're using a headless browser to do the testing.
speaker: SPEAKER_00

text: but that is what it is.
speaker: SPEAKER_00

text: Now, a lot of times with end-to-end tests, depending upon the company and how they want to do it, there might be an actual server that's fired up just for that environment, just so it can run and test that environment.
speaker: SPEAKER_00

text: However, anytime there's any external dependencies involved, tests do become very flaky.
speaker: SPEAKER_00

text: For whatever reason, the network just decides to time out and now your test is broke.
speaker: SPEAKER_00

text: These can break down your confidence of your system.
speaker: SPEAKER_00

text: So what ends up happening a lot of times with end to end tests is it'll be an end to end test all the way up until the actual application needs to leave its boundary.
speaker: SPEAKER_00

text: That boundary is going to be the network.
speaker: SPEAKER_00

text: It's going to be the file system or whatever.
speaker: SPEAKER_00

text: And at that point, certain mocks will take over or fakes say, Hey, this is a fake API.
speaker: SPEAKER_00

text: And it returns a set of data that's expected in that scenario.
speaker: SPEAKER_00

text: This will make it very hermetic.
speaker: SPEAKER_00

text: So we can actually just repeat these tests over and over and we don't have to worry about the actual network going down or anything of that nature.
speaker: SPEAKER_00

text: These will make it much more reliable, which then in turn will increase your confidence that you did not break anything when you wrote your code.
speaker: SPEAKER_00

text: So to recap, number two, make sure you know how to write tests and what the different types of tests are.
speaker: SPEAKER_00

text: This includes unit tests, integration tests, and end-to-end tests.
speaker: SPEAKER_00

text: Okay, number three.
speaker: SPEAKER_00

text: This one is going to be, I fought with this one internally, but in my opinion, this one is very important strictly because in my experience with it, it has made me so valuable as an employee.
speaker: SPEAKER_00

text: And as a freelancer and as a consultant, it's something that's on my resume that when I come into a client, I can instantly make their development teams and environments just so much stronger and so much more professional with this simple thing.
speaker: SPEAKER_00

text: And number three is you need to know how to set up a continuous integration server.
speaker: SPEAKER_00

text: So a continuous integration server, what is it?
speaker: SPEAKER_00

text: It allows you, when you check in your code to your version control system, which we're going to assume is Git, when you check that code in, the continuous integration server sits off to the side up in the cloud somewhere or internal in your network and watches that repository and says, oh, hey, there was some new code pushed to that.
speaker: SPEAKER_00

text: Let me pull that down.
speaker: SPEAKER_00

text: And it's an automated thing.
speaker: SPEAKER_00

text: Let me pull that code down and I'm going to go ahead and build it and run all the tests.
speaker: SPEAKER_00

text: And I'm going to see if, make sure everything is good.
speaker: SPEAKER_00

text: And if it's not, then I'm going to send out some alerts to some people, usually through email or maybe a chat message or something like that.
speaker: SPEAKER_00

text: So it continues, you need to know how to set these things up.
speaker: SPEAKER_00

text: Now there's different services, which I'll cover in a minute and different self-hosted options that I'll talk about as well.
speaker: SPEAKER_00

text: But the bare minimum that you need to know how to do as a developer, freelancer, consultant, whatever, is you need to make sure that you can set up the continuous integration server.
speaker: SPEAKER_00

text: Then you need to make sure that it can actually build your apps.
speaker: SPEAKER_00

text: Now, that's if your app is compilable.
speaker: SPEAKER_00

text: If it's a compiled language like Java or C Sharp or something like that.
speaker: SPEAKER_00

text: You'll want to make sure that your build server will execute that build script to create the actual binary.
speaker: SPEAKER_00

text: So that's step one.
speaker: SPEAKER_00

text: Then number two, you need to make sure that it will run all of your tests, which would be step two.
speaker: SPEAKER_00

text: So compile, then run all of your tests.
speaker: SPEAKER_00

text: Again, if you're running a interpreted language, maybe JavaScript, Ruby, Python, something like that, and you're not going to actually build it, you want to make sure that it's installing all of your dependencies beforehand.
speaker: SPEAKER_00

text: So it's ready.
speaker: SPEAKER_00

text: And then you want to run all of your test suites after that.
speaker: SPEAKER_00

text: Now this is going to include your unit tests, your integration tests and your system tests.
speaker: SPEAKER_00

text: It needs to run all of those.
speaker: SPEAKER_00

text: And this is going to happen on every single one of your commits.
speaker: SPEAKER_00

text: Now we'll get to the testing here in a second, but just assume we're running all of them.
speaker: SPEAKER_00

text: Now, finally, after and if the build succeeds, which is the compilation of your binary and the tests succeed as well, the test suites are all passing.
speaker: SPEAKER_00

text: At that point, it's gonna deploy the artifacts of your actual build.
speaker: SPEAKER_00

text: And that's going to be usually your binary and perhaps any of the reports from your tests that you have.
speaker: SPEAKER_00

text: That'll deploy off to a folder somewhere.
speaker: SPEAKER_00

text: Very often it's just right there on the build server or it could be on like an S3 bucket or whatever.
speaker: SPEAKER_00

text: Just somewhere where you can get to it is all that matters at this point.
speaker: SPEAKER_00

text: You can optimize that later.
speaker: SPEAKER_00

text: And at that point, that's the bare minimum that you need to know how to do.
speaker: SPEAKER_00

text: And of course, there's going to be alerts that are set up that should kind of be a general thing.
speaker: SPEAKER_00

text: So maybe we'll add that as an additional one is make sure that the alerts are set up.
speaker: SPEAKER_00

text: So if it does break, it emails all the team members.
speaker: SPEAKER_00

text: That's the bare minimum.
speaker: SPEAKER_00

text: Now, there are many advanced features of continuous integration servers.
speaker: SPEAKER_00

text: That's going to be such thing as like auto deployment.
speaker: SPEAKER_00

text: If you have a mobile app, maybe it auto deploys itself to Google Play or the app store based upon certain criteria.
speaker: SPEAKER_00

text: Or if it's a web app automatically deploys itself to Heroku or whatever your hosting services, S3, I mean, AWS or anything like that.
speaker: SPEAKER_00

text: There is also a ton of other advanced CI CD pipelines, which is continuous integration, continuous delivery pipelines that you can get into.
speaker: SPEAKER_00

text: However, again, those are advanced.
speaker: SPEAKER_00

text: I'm not talking about advanced stuff right here.
speaker: SPEAKER_00

text: The bare minimum we're talking about is compiling your app, making sure your dependencies are installed, running in all of your tests, deploying the artifacts, and then any type of notifications for build failures.
speaker: SPEAKER_00

text: Now, why is this important?
speaker: SPEAKER_00

text: Because very often I have found when I go to companies, usually small companies, there's one or two developers there and they work together.
speaker: SPEAKER_00

text: They build the application themselves.
speaker: SPEAKER_00

text: There's not really problems.
speaker: SPEAKER_00

text: They don't need a built server.
speaker: SPEAKER_00

text: However, once your team grows, even beyond one person, really, you can run into the situation, and I'm sure you have, where someone changes something and it works on their machine, but it doesn't work on yours.
speaker: SPEAKER_00

text: Why not?
speaker: SPEAKER_00

text: Well, maybe they changed something and forgot to check it in.
speaker: SPEAKER_00

text: The build server is going to be your source of truth.
speaker: SPEAKER_00

text: It's going to actually be the one who checks everything that's checked into your code base to verify that, hey, this does still build all of our test pass and everything is good.
speaker: SPEAKER_00

text: Now, if it doesn't, if it can't complete all of those steps.
speaker: SPEAKER_00

text: What it does is it fails and lets everyone know, hey, looks like this build failed.
speaker: SPEAKER_00

text: Now, if you've used GitHub or Bitbucket or GitLab, you've seen a lot of these build systems already built into these platforms and you'll see a lot of open source libraries will have builds enabled and you may have seen a failed build here or there.
speaker: SPEAKER_00

text: What that allows you to do on these platforms, which we'll get to in a second, is dive into what was broken.
speaker: SPEAKER_00

text: You might find that a unit test broke or an integration test broke.
speaker: SPEAKER_00

text: These are going to be the safety mechanisms for you to realize like, oh,
speaker: SPEAKER_00

text: I changed some code in this function.
speaker: SPEAKER_00

text: I didn't know it was used by this other place over here.
speaker: SPEAKER_00

text: And I can't return that value because, oh, you know, and you just kind of go down that whole path of realizing what happened and then you can dive into it and fix it.
speaker: SPEAKER_00

text: This was...
speaker: SPEAKER_00

text: Catching your errors this early on is much cheaper than catching them in production.
speaker: SPEAKER_00

text: You have to trust me on it.
speaker: SPEAKER_00

text: Catching them in production is the most costliest mistake you can have.
speaker: SPEAKER_00

text: So a continuous integration server is going to save you and your company time, money, stress, effort, everything.
speaker: SPEAKER_00

text: Yes, it's going to take a little bit of time to set up.
speaker: SPEAKER_00

text: Yes, it might be something new for you to learn.
speaker: SPEAKER_00

text: However, if you can set up a continuous integration server and build it and get it running with the proper build scripts, you become very valuable to your team.
speaker: SPEAKER_00

text: You might be the person that they rely on for all the build stuff.
speaker: SPEAKER_00

text: That could be a good or bad thing if you don't like it.
speaker: SPEAKER_00

text: However, as you progress through your career, this should almost be second nature for you.
speaker: SPEAKER_00

text: If you show up to a client and they don't have it, just set one up.
speaker: SPEAKER_00

text: For me, that's exactly what I do.
speaker: SPEAKER_00

text: In fact, if I go to a client and they need me to build them a web app or a mobile app, I ask if they have continuous integration.
speaker: SPEAKER_00

text: Half the time, they don't even know what I'm talking about.
speaker: SPEAKER_00

text: So I'll just set one up for them on one of the free services that I'm going to list here below.
speaker: SPEAKER_00

text: If they have one already set up, then I'll use theirs or I'll have their team set it up for me if they have a team for that.
speaker: SPEAKER_00

text: So this is going to change for each different platform you're working on.
speaker: SPEAKER_00

text: If you're building an Android app, you need, you know, Gradle scripts.
speaker: SPEAKER_00

text: You need to know how to run Espresso emulators, stuff like that.
speaker: SPEAKER_00

text: If you're working in Ruby or Rails, you'll need to know how to work with, you know, RSpec or Minitest or anything like that and headless browser testing.
speaker: SPEAKER_00

text: So it just changes depending upon the platform you're working in.
speaker: SPEAKER_00

text: But there's tons of information that you can Google on how to set one up.
speaker: SPEAKER_00

text: Now, what are the different services that you could use to set these up to have experience?
speaker: SPEAKER_00

text: Any of the version control companies that are out there, the big ones, have build servers built into them now.
speaker: SPEAKER_00

text: Now, it always wasn't like this, but now it is, which is awesome.
speaker: SPEAKER_00

text: So if you're on GitHub, you can use their build servers.
speaker: SPEAKER_00

text: They have GitLab has build servers, Bitbucket, Azure DevOps.
speaker: SPEAKER_00

text: So those are kind of like the big ones that you're going to have there right out of the gate.
speaker: SPEAKER_00

text: So if you're hosting your code anywhere in there, you can most likely, and I do, use their build services just simply because it's already easy to set up.
speaker: SPEAKER_00

text: It's already integrated into the platform, and I don't have to worry about additional work of...
speaker: SPEAKER_00

text: firing up another server on a VPC somewhere and managing that whole thing.
speaker: SPEAKER_00

text: I don't want to do that.
speaker: SPEAKER_00

text: I want less work.
speaker: SPEAKER_00

text: Now, there also are other third-party services that are outside of these platforms, which I've used as well.
speaker: SPEAKER_00

text: Companies like CircleCI or Bitrise or CodeMagic.
speaker: SPEAKER_00

text: There's a bunch more out there.
speaker: SPEAKER_00

text: You could Google build server for whatever your technology is.
speaker: SPEAKER_00

text: You're going to find a ton of them.
speaker: SPEAKER_00

text: But like the ones I mentioned here, CircleCI, Bitrise, CodeMagic, et cetera, you can go there and they'll actually plug into your version control system.
speaker: SPEAKER_00

text: And some of them provide different types of things such as, hey, this is one supports Flutter and this one doesn't.
speaker: SPEAKER_00

text: And this one supports Erlang and that one doesn't.
speaker: SPEAKER_00

text: So you just kind of have to look around for what you like.
speaker: SPEAKER_00

text: And those are the services that are hosted outside of the version control platforms.
speaker: SPEAKER_00

text: So you can use one of those as well.
speaker: SPEAKER_00

text: Now, if you are in a situation in which you need a self-hosted solution, in that situation, you're gonna wanna make sure you know how to install and configure your own.
speaker: SPEAKER_00

text: This is a lot more work, a lot more intensive.
speaker: SPEAKER_00

text: However, if you can do it, again, you're gonna be much more valuable to your client or to your employer.
speaker: SPEAKER_00

text: And what are the self-hosted options?
speaker: SPEAKER_00

text: Again, there's many, but I'm only going to recommend two here because these are the two that I've used in depth.
speaker: SPEAKER_00

text: Number one is Jenkins.
speaker: SPEAKER_00

text: This is the industry standard.
speaker: SPEAKER_00

text: You're going to see all over the place.
speaker: SPEAKER_00

text: If they're not using a built-in service, most likely a company is using Jenkins.
speaker: SPEAKER_00

text: The only other option to that that I've seen a lot is a self-hosted TeamCity installation, and that's by JetBrains.
speaker: SPEAKER_00

text: Now, again, there's actually a bunch of other ones out there.
speaker: SPEAKER_00

text: I've used ones like cruisecontrol.net for many, many years ago when I was doing .NET development.
speaker: SPEAKER_00

text: I don't even know if that's still a thing, but there's many of them out there.
speaker: SPEAKER_00

text: But if you're going to self-host, look at something like Jenkins.
speaker: SPEAKER_00

text: That's literally the power horse of self-hosted build servers for continuous integration.
speaker: SPEAKER_00

text: Or you can take a look at something like TeamCity.
speaker: SPEAKER_00

text: So when you have the ability to set up a continuous integration server, you're just going to end up being more valuable at your company.
speaker: SPEAKER_00

text: You're going to understand more of the process from start to finish of building the software and managing a software development team and lifecycle.
speaker: SPEAKER_00

text: It's just going to help you out in the end in your career.
speaker: SPEAKER_00

text: So that's it.
speaker: SPEAKER_00

text: Those are the top three things every software developer needs to know how to do.
speaker: SPEAKER_00

text: Let's quickly recap them.
speaker: SPEAKER_00

text: Number one, you need to know how to identify and avoid premature optimization.
speaker: SPEAKER_00

text: Number two, you need to know how to write tests and what the different types of tests are.
speaker: SPEAKER_00

text: This includes unit tests, integration tests and end-to-end tests.
speaker: SPEAKER_00

text: And number three, you need to know how to set up and configure a continuous integration server so that it builds your apps, installs your dependencies, runs all of your tests and deploys the artifacts and notifies anyone of any build failures.
speaker: SPEAKER_00

text: Now, there's a ton of other things that software developers need to know how to do.
speaker: SPEAKER_00

text: But in my opinion, these are some of the things that can give you an immediate benefit to your career over the long term of whatever you're working in and whatever industry you're working in.
speaker: SPEAKER_00

text: These things will always be valuable and have been valuable for me for close to two decades now.
speaker: SPEAKER_00

text: I hope that stuff helps.
speaker: SPEAKER_00

text: And again, if you guys have any questions, feel free to go ahead and send me an email or send me a message on any of these social media platforms.
